questions.explanation: The .xcodeproj contains the project structure, targets, build settings, and code signing configuration used by Xcode.
questions.question: In Xcode, which file typically defines an iOS app’s build settings, targets, and signing configuration?

questions.explanation: Git is the dominant version control system used to track changes, collaborate, and manage releases in software projects.
questions.question: Which tool is most commonly used for software configuration management (version control) in iOS projects?

questions.explanation: 'let' declares a constant whose value cannot be changed after initialization.
questions.question: In Swift, what does the keyword 'let' declare?

questions.explanation: Flip-flops are clocked storage elements that hold a single bit, forming the basis of registers and sequential logic.

options.B: Store one bit of state synchronized to a clock

questions.question: In digital systems, what is the primary purpose of a flip-flop?

questions.explanation: A leading-to-superview constraint with a constant of 16 directly expresses the required margin and adapts to different screen sizes.

options.A: Set the label’s frame in viewDidLoad and never use constraints
options.B: Add a leading constraint from the label to the superview with constant 16
options.C: Set the label’s content hugging priority to 1000
options.D: Use a centerX constraint and set the label’s width to 16

questions.question: You need a UILabel to keep a fixed 16pt margin from the leading edge of its superview across all device sizes. Which Auto Layout approach is most appropriate?

questions.explanation: PUT is typically used to replace/update a resource at a known URI using the full representation.
questions.question: Which HTTP method is most appropriate for updating an existing resource (e.g., /devices/42) in a RESTful API when sending the full updated representation?

questions.explanation: MainActor.run guarantees the enclosed code executes on the main actor (main thread), which is required for UIKit/SwiftUI UI updates.

options.A: Call sleep(1) before updating the UI
options.B: Wrap the UI update in DispatchQueue.global().async

questions.question: In Swift concurrency, what is the correct way to ensure a UI update happens on the main thread after awaiting a network call?

questions.explanation: Modeling readings as entities with queryable attributes enables efficient filtered fetches and sorting, supporting offline access and scalable datasets.

options.A: Store all readings as a single JSON blob in UserDefaults
options.B: Use a Core Data entity 'Reading' with indexed attributes (timestamp, sensorID) and fetch with NSPredicate + sort descriptors
options.C: Write readings to a plist file and load the entire file into memory for every query
options.D: Avoid persistence and re-fetch from the device each time

questions.question: You are building an iOS companion app for an embedded device. The app must cache sensor readings locally and support offline viewing with efficient queries (e.g., last 24 hours, by sensor ID). Which Core Data design best fits this requirement?

questions.explanation: Stubbing via URLProtocol or injecting a mock client isolates the ViewModel from the network, allowing deterministic tests of decoding and mapping with async expectations.

options.A: Run the test only on a physical device connected to the embedded hardware
options.B: Use URLProtocol stubbing (or a mocked networking layer) to return deterministic JSON, then assert ViewModel outputs using XCTest expectations
options.C: Add a 5-second delay and hope the network response arrives before assertions
options.D: Disable ATS (App Transport Security) so tests run faster

questions.question: Your app uses MVVM and fetches device status via URLSession. You want a unit test that verifies decoding and state mapping without making real network calls. What is the best approach with XCTest?

questions.explanation: Uploading to App Store Connect/TestFlight requires correct code signing: a distribution certificate and appropriate provisioning profile (or configured automatic signing with the right team permissions).

options.A: They did not add Auto Layout constraints to all views
options.B: They are missing a valid distribution certificate and an App Store provisioning profile (or proper automatic signing access) for the app’s bundle ID
options.C: They used SwiftUI instead of UIKit
options.D: They did not include a Core Data model file

questions.question: You are preparing an iOS app for TestFlight distribution to field-test an embedded device integration. A teammate can build locally but cannot upload to App Store Connect. Which is the most likely missing requirement?
